6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

So, There is new method of REST API which is QUERY.
Before we start: what even is a “REST API method”?
If you’re new to this, let’s build the foundation first.
When your browser or app talks to a server, it needs to tell the server what it wants to do. That “what” is expressed using an HTTP method (also loosely called a “REST method” when used in REST APIs).
Think methods as verbs and url as nouns
GET /products : get me the products
POST /products : create a new productHere are main HTTP methods you will meet in almost every REST API:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Quick definitions you’ll need throughout this article:

• Safe → The request does not change anything on the server. It’s a pure “read.” Calling it 1 time or 100 times has zero side effects.

• Idempotent → Calling the same request multiple times gives the same result / same end-state as calling it once. No extra side effects pile up.

• Cacheable → The response can be stored (cached) by browsers, CDNs, or proxies, and reused for future identical requests — making things faster.
Now let’s continue with why we need this new QUERY method.
What is the problem with GET method?
GET is the method for reading data and it is safe, idempotent and cacheable.
but twist is GET has no defined way to carry a request body.
so everything you wanted to search for has to be written into URL as query params as per below:
GET /products?category=Laptop&price_gte=50000&brand=Apple&brand=Dell&sort=price&order=descThis is okay, but not for real-time production search and filter systems get messy because of below things:
URL length limits: different systems, servers and proxies have some limits of different max URL lengths. Push past that with a complex filter and requests start failing unpredictably.
No nested or complex structures: Try representing something like { "price": { "gte": 50000, "lte": 90000 } } cleanly in a query string. You end up with ugly, error-prone syntax like price[gte]=50000&price[lte]=90000, and every framework encodes this a bit differently.
Logging & privacy leaks: URLs (including everything in the query string) are commonly logged by servers, proxies, browser history, and analytics tools. Sensitive filter data sitting in the URL is more exposed than data sitting in a request body.
Encoding headaches: Special characters, arrays, and Unicode all need URL-encoding, which becomes a nightmare for deeply structured filters.
In short : GET is correct for reading data but technically limited for complex readds.
So now this leads to another question which is as per below:
So why not just use POST for search?
This is what most APIs have been doing for years. think POST /search, POST /products/query, GraphQL's POST /graphql, or Elasticsearch's POST /_search.
POST solves the body-size and structure problem perfectly. You can send any JSON payload, however deeply nested, however large. So what’s the issue?
Issue is semantics : POST is lying about what he is doing.
Now let’s see according to HTTP’s rules: (Right now see above definition which I mentioned before starting with GET method).
POST is not safe : protocol thinks that it chages server state.
POST is not idemoptent : protocol thinks that if I call this twice it creates duplicates.
POST response are not cached by default : because caches can’t assume POST is pure read.
So when you use POST for search or fetch data remember:
browsers, proxies or any CDNs treats as it is cautiously.
you are technically misusing the method just because it happens to support a body.
It works, but it’s a workaround, not a correct solution. This is the exact gap the industry has felt for over a decade — and it’s why a new method was proposed.
Entry of QUERY method
In June 2026, the IETF (the organization that standardizes core internet protocols like HTTP) officially published RFC 10008 — “The HTTP QUERY Method” as an approved Internet Standard.
It was authored by engineers from greenbytes, Cloudflare, and Akamai.
What QUERY actually does:
QUERY takes POST’s ability to carry a body, and combines it with GET’s safe, idempotent, cacheable behavior.
QUERY /products HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
Content-Type: application/json{
"category": "Laptop",
"price": {
"gte": 50000
},
"brand": ["Apple", "Dell"]
}
The server processes the JSON body and responds just like a GET would — with the matching results:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json{
"results": [
{ "id": 101, "name": "MacBook Air", "brand": "Apple", "price": 92000 },
{ "id": 108, "name": "XPS 13", "brand": "Dell", "price": 78000 }
],
"count": 2
}
same URL structure and same JSON body style as POST — but the protocol-level guarantees of GET.
Why QUERY and not SEARCH?
Earlier drafts actually experimented with names borrowed from the old WebDAV method family — SEARCH, PROPFIND, REPORT. The working group eventually settled on QUERY because it maps naturally to the “query” part of a URL and represents a generic safe-read operation, not a narrow “text search” use case.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How does QUERY actually work under the hood?

• Content negotiation matters. The request must include a proper Content-Type header (like application/json). If it's missing or doesn't match the actual body, the server returns a 4xx error.

• Discoverability via Accept-Query. A server can advertise that it supports QUERY — and which content types it accepts — using a new response header called Accept-Query. This means a client doesn't have to blindly guess whether an endpoint supports QUERY.
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Accept-Query: application/json, application/x-www-form-urlencoded
• New-ish status code usage. If the body’s content type is fine but the query itself can’t be processed (say, a filter references a field that doesn’t exist), the server can respond with 422 Unprocessable Content — the same status code used elsewhere in HTTP for “I understood your syntax, but I can’t act on it.”

• Caching needs the full body as part of the cache key. Because two QUERY requests to the same URL can have completely different bodies (and therefore completely different results), a cache must hash/include the request body when deciding whether a cached response can be reused. Get this wrong, and you open the door to cache poisoning or cache deception bugs — this is a genuinely new engineering responsibility that didn’t exist with plain GET caching.

• QUERY is not “safelisted” for CORS. In browsers, only a small set of methods and headers can bypass a CORS preflight check. QUERY isn’t on that list, so browser-based QUERY requests will trigger an OPTIONS preflight, just like most POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE requests already do.
Real world use cases of query:
QUERY is aimed squarely at situations where you need to read data using a complex, structured request — not simple key-value filters. Some strong fits:

• E-commerce filtering — multi-field filters like price ranges, multiple brands, nested category trees (like the laptop example above).

• Analytics and reporting dashboards — sending a complex query object (date ranges, group-bys, multiple metrics) to fetch a report.

• Search-heavy platforms — anything currently using POST /search, like Elasticsearch-style APIs, can migrate to a semantically correct QUERY.

• AI-powered search / RAG-style endpoints — sending structured prompts, filters, and parameters to retrieve relevant documents or results.

• GraphQL-style flexible queries over plain REST — without needing a full GraphQL layer.

• APIs behind CDNs — since QUERY is explicitly cacheable, CDNs (like Cloudflare and Akamai, who literally co-authored the RFC) can cache complex read requests that were previously stuck behind uncacheable POST calls.
Framework support:

• Node.js — has been able to parse the QUERY method at the HTTP layer since early 2024, ahead of formal standardization.

• Express.js — support for the QUERY method has already landed in Express, so app.query('/products', handler)-style routing is becoming available in the ecosystem.
Sources

• RFC 10008 — The HTTP QUERY Method, IETF, June 2026 (rfc-editor.org)

• IETF HTTP Working Group draft history (datatracker.ietf.org)

• Community and security analysis on RFC 10008 adoption, mid-2026
